content.content:
1	# `@shardworks/cartograph-apparatus`
2	
3	The Cartograph stands up the **decomposition-ladder** substrate: a typed
4	data layer for tracking long-lived patron intent across four levels —
5	**vision** (top, patron-owned, long-lived) → **charge** (first
6	decomposition, the unit of patron walkthrough) → **piece** (recursive,
7	internal organization, self-nesting) → **mandate** (the existing leaf
8	where rigs attach).
9	
10	This commission lands the **data-and-typed-API substrate plus a
11	patron-facing CLI**. There is no agent runtime and no downstream
12	consumer in this package — those land in follow-on commissions. What
13	ships here:
14	
15	- Three new writ types contributed to the Clerk: `vision`, `charge`,
16	  `piece`. Each uses a six-state mandate-clone lifecycle (no
17	  `childrenBehavior` cascade — patron-walkthrough semantics will be
18	  coordinated by the typed API and downstream consumers, not by
19	  registry-side cascade rules).
20	- Three companion books (`visions`, `charges`, `pieces`) keyed by the
21	  writ id, each holding a typed companion document with the per-type
22	  stage and audit fields.
23	- A `CartographApi` exposed via `provides` that is the **only** layer
24	  enforcing the ladder's parent invariants:
25	  - vision has no parent;
26	  - `charge.parentId` must be a vision;
27	  - `piece.parentId` must be a charge or piece.
28	  Raw `clerk.post({ type: 'vision' })` continues to succeed without
29	  parent-type checks — the typed API is the validator. The mandate-side
30	  rules (mandate may attach under any non-terminal node) stay where they
31	  are.
32	- A patron-facing CLI surface contributed via `supportKit.tools` —
33	  three subcommand groups (`nsg vision`, `nsg charge`, `nsg piece`),
34	  each with five operations (`create`, `show`, `list`, `patch`,
35	  `transition`) plus the on-disk authoring tool `nsg vision apply`.
36	  The framework `nsg` auto-builder discovers the tools at startup and
37	  groups them by hyphen prefix; no edits to the framework CLI package
38	  are required. Every tool routes through the typed API above, so the
39	  parent invariants and lifecycle coupling hold for CLI-driven
40	  authoring.
41	
42	The Cartograph requires `stacks` and `clerk` and recommends `oculus`. The
43	Oculus writs page automatically renders the new types via the
44	type-vocabulary helper; without oculus the data is invisible to the
45	dashboard.
46	
47	---
48	
49	## Installation
50	
51	```json
52	{
53	  "dependencies": {
54	    "@shardworks/cartograph-apparatus": "workspace:*"
55	  }
56	}
57	```
58	
59	## API
60	
61	The Cartograph exposes a `CartographApi` via `provides`, accessible at
62	runtime via `guild().apparatus<CartographApi>('cartograph')`.
63	
64	```typescript
65	import type { CartographApi } from '@shardworks/cartograph-apparatus';
66	
67	const cartograph = guild().apparatus<CartographApi>('cartograph');
68	
69	// Create a vision (no parent permitted)
70	const vision = await cartograph.createVision({
71	  title: 'Land the agentic decomposition ladder',
72	  body: 'Long-form patron intent ...',
73	  codex: 'main',
74	});
75	
76	// Create a charge under that vision
77	const charge = await cartograph.createCharge({
78	  parentId: vision.id,
79	  title: 'Stand up the data substrate',
80	  body: 'First decomposition ...',
81	});
82	
83	// Create a piece under the charge
84	const piece = await cartograph.createPiece({
85	  parentId: charge.id,
86	  title: 'Pick a companion-doc shape',
87	  body: 'Internal organization ...',
88	});
89	
90	// Pieces can self-nest
91	const subPiece = await cartograph.createPiece({
92	  parentId: piece.id,
93	  title: 'Decide how createX writes both rows atomically',
94	  body: 'Internal sub-piece ...',
95	});
96	```
97	
98	Each `createX` opens a single `stacks.transaction(...)` and writes the
99	writ row and the companion doc inside one atomic boundary. Parent
100	existence, parent-not-terminal, codex inheritance, and id generation
101	match Clerk's `post()` validation byte-for-byte (the duplication is the
102	cost of being a typed atomic surface).
103	
104	### `CartographApi`
105	
106	```typescript
107	interface CartographApi {
108	  // Vision
109	  createVision(req: CreateVisionRequest): Promise<VisionDoc>;
110	  showVision(id: string): Promise<VisionDoc>;
111	  listVisions(filters?: VisionFilters): Promise<VisionDoc[]>;
112	  patchVision(id: string, fields: Partial<Omit<VisionDoc, 'id'>>): Promise<VisionDoc>;
113	  transitionVision(id: string, to: { phase: WritPhase; stage: VisionStage; resolution?: string }): Promise<VisionDoc>;
114	
115	  // Charge
116	  createCharge(req: CreateChargeRequest): Promise<ChargeDoc>;
117	  showCharge(id: string): Promise<ChargeDoc>;
118	  listCharges(filters?: ChargeFilters): Promise<ChargeDoc[]>;
119	  patchCharge(id: string, fields: Partial<Omit<ChargeDoc, 'id'>>): Promise<ChargeDoc>;
120	  transitionCharge(id: string, to: { phase: WritPhase; stage: ChargeStage; resolution?: string }): Promise<ChargeDoc>;
121	
122	  // Piece
123	  createPiece(req: CreatePieceRequest): Promise<PieceDoc>;
124	  showPiece(id: string): Promise<PieceDoc>;
125	  listPieces(filters?: PieceFilters): Promise<PieceDoc[]>;
126	  patchPiece(id: string, fields: Partial<Omit<PieceDoc, 'id'>>): Promise<PieceDoc>;
127	  transitionPiece(id: string, to: { phase: WritPhase; stage: PieceStage; resolution?: string }): Promise<PieceDoc>;
128	}
129	```
130	
131	The lifecycle-coupled `transitionX` methods update both `writ.phase` and
132	the companion doc's `stage` field atomically, inside a single Stacks
133	transaction. The caller specifies both the target phase and the target
134	stage explicitly because a single phase may map to multiple stages
135	depending on context (e.g. a charge moving to `failed` could mean
136	stage `dropped` or stage `validated` depending on outcome).
137	
138	### Companion documents
139	
140	```typescript
141	interface VisionDoc { id: string; stage: VisionStage; codex?: string; createdAt: string; updatedAt: string; }
142	interface ChargeDoc { id: string; stage: ChargeStage; codex?: string; createdAt: string; updatedAt: string; }
143	interface PieceDoc  { id: string; stage: PieceStage;  codex?: string; createdAt: string; updatedAt: string; }
144	
145	type VisionStage = 'draft' | 'active' | 'sunset' | 'cancelled';
146	type ChargeStage = 'draft' | 'active' | 'validated' | 'dropped';
147	type PieceStage  = 'draft' | 'active' | 'done' | 'dropped';
148	```
149	
150	The minimal field set is deliberate — the patch surface plus the
151	`[key: string]: unknown` index signature lets consumers grow the field
152	set non-breakingly later. Vision text lives on `writ.body`; the
153	companion doc carries typed metadata only.
154	
155	### Filters
156	
157	`VisionFilters`, `ChargeFilters`, and `PieceFilters` mirror the
158	`PlanFilters` shape from astrolabe — `{ stage?, codex?, limit?, offset? }`.
159	Lists are ordered by `createdAt desc` (newest first).
160	
161	## Support Kit
162	
163	### Books
164	
165	| Book | Owner | Indexes |
166	|---|---|---|
167	| `visions` | `cartograph` | `stage`, `codex`, `createdAt` |
168	| `charges` | `cartograph` | `stage`, `codex`, `createdAt` |
169	| `pieces`  | `cartograph` | `stage`, `codex`, `createdAt` |
170	
171	### Writ Types (contributed to Clerk)
172	
173	| Name | Description |
174	|---|---|
175	| `vision` | Top-level patron intent. No parent. Long-lived. |
176	| `charge` | First decomposition under a vision; the unit of patron walkthrough. Parent must be a vision. |
177	| `piece`  | Recursive internal organization. Self-nests. Parent must be a charge or piece. |
178	
179	Each writ type uses a six-state mandate-clone lifecycle:
180	`new` (initial) → `open` (active) → `stuck`/`completed`/`failed`/`cancelled`;
181	no `childrenBehavior` cascade.
182	
183	## CLI
184	
185	Cartograph contributes three subcommand groups to `nsg` — one per writ
186	type — with the same five operations under each:
187	
188	| Command | Description |
189	|---|---|
190	| `nsg vision create --title <t> --body <b> [--codex <c>]` | Create a top-level vision (writ at `phase: new`, doc at `stage: draft`). |
191	| `nsg charge create --parent-id <vision> --title <t> --body <b> [--codex <c>]` | Create a charge under a vision. |
192	| `nsg piece create --parent-id <charge\|piece> --title <t> --body <b> [--codex <c>]` | Create a piece under a charge or piece (self-nests). |
193	| `nsg <type> show <id> [--format text\|json]` | Show the companion doc joined with the writ row. Text mode mirrors `nsg writ show`'s lifecycle-aware block; JSON returns `{ ...doc, writ: { ... } }`. |
194	| `nsg <type> list [--stage <s>] [--codex <c>] [--limit <n>] [--offset <n>] [--format text\|json]` | Tabular list (STAGE \| ID \| CODEX \| TITLE \| CREATED) ordered by `createdAt desc`. |
195	| `nsg <type> patch <id> --codex <c>` | Patch the companion doc's `codex`. Title and body live on the writ row — edit them via `nsg writ edit`. |
196	| `nsg <type> transition <id> --phase <writ-phase> --stage <doc-stage> [--resolution <r>]` | Atomically advance both `writ.phase` and the companion doc's `stage` inside one Stacks transaction. Both `--phase` and `--stage` are required because a single phase may map to multiple stages depending on context. |
197	| `nsg vision apply <slug> [--severity <s>] [--deadline <d>] [--decay <d>]` | Snapshot the on-disk vision at `<GUILD>/vision/<slug>/` into a vision writ + VisionDoc. Idempotent: first apply creates and binds; later applies sync the file's contents into the bound writ. CLI flags override sidecar values for the surveyor priority-hint payload. |
198	
199	The CLI tools are read or write according to operation: `show`/`list`
200	declare `permission: 'read'`; `create`/`patch`/`transition`/`apply`
201	declare `permission: 'write'`. Every tool declares
202	`callableBy: ['patron']` — they are not exposed to anima or library
203	callers in this commission. Only `show` and `list` accept the
204	`--format text|json` flag; write tools return their result directly
205	and the framework auto-stringifies.
206	
207	Short-prefix id resolution (via `clerk.resolveId`) works on every
208	id-bearing flag: `nsg vision show w-mo123` and
209	`nsg charge create --parent-id w-mo123` both succeed when the prefix
210	matches a single writ.
211	
212	## On-disk vision authoring
213	
214	Visions are prose-heavy and long-lived; the patron-facing path is to
215	author them in a normal editor on disk and snapshot the directory into
216	the cartograph with `nsg vision apply <slug>`. The same code path
217	handles first-time creation and Nth re-import — the binding is durable
218	across applies, so the file tree is the source of truth for vision
219	content while the cartograph remains the source of truth for lifecycle
220	state and ext metadata.
221	
222	### File layout
223	
224	```
225	<GUILD>/
226	└── vision/
227	    └── <slug>/
228	        ├── vision.md              # long-form vision text → writ.body
229	        └── vision-metadata.yml    # required sidecar
230	```
231	
232	The slug must be a single directory name (lowercase letters, digits,
233	hyphens, or underscores; no path separators, no leading dots, no
234	`..`). It is fixed at creation time; rename is not supported.
235	
236	### Sidecar shape
237	
238	```yaml
239	# vision-metadata.yml
240	title: Land the agentic decomposition ladder    # required
241	stage: draft                                    # required: draft | active
242	
243	codex: main                                     # optional
244	severity: high                                  # optional priority hint
245	deadline: 2026-06-30                            # optional priority hint
246	decay: slow                                     # optional priority hint
247	complexity: medium                              # optional priority hint (sidecar-only)
248	resolution: patron retired the vision           # optional, used on terminal transitions
249	
250	# Managed by `nsg vision apply` — do not edit by hand.
251	visionId: w-...                                 # written after first apply
252	```
253	
254	| Field | Required | Notes |
255	|---|---|---|
256	| `title` | yes | Short human-readable title for the vision writ. |
257	| `stage` | yes | Lifecycle stage. Allowed initial values are `draft` (writ phase `new`) and `active` (writ phase `open`). `sunset` and `cancelled` are rejected on first apply — a vision cannot be born retired; transition to a terminal stage on a later apply. |
258	| `codex` | no | Target codex name for the vision. |
259	| `visionId` | no, system-managed | Written by `apply` after first creation. **Do not edit by hand.** Clear the field only to deliberately re-create the vision (the old writ stays in place and becomes orphaned from this directory). |
260	| `severity` | no | Priority hint surfaced in `writ.ext['surveyor']`. |
261	| `deadline` | no | Priority hint surfaced in `writ.ext['surveyor']`. |
262	| `decay` | no | Priority hint surfaced in `writ.ext['surveyor']`. |
263	| `complexity` | no | Priority hint surfaced in `writ.ext['surveyor']`. Sidecar-only — there is no CLI flag for this field. |
264	| `resolution` | no | Passed through to `transitionVision` on terminal moves. |
265	
266	Unknown keys are logged as a warning and ignored (preserves
267	forward-compatibility while still surfacing typos).
268	
269	### `visionId` binding
270	
271	After the first successful `nsg vision apply <slug>`, the tool writes
272	the new writ's id back into the sidecar as `visionId`. The yaml is
273	round-tripped via `parseDocument`, so comments and key order survive
274	the rewrite, and the file is replaced atomically (write-temp,
275	rename-over) so a process crash cannot lose the binding.
276	
277	On every subsequent apply the tool resolves the writ via `visionId`,
278	updates `writ.body` from `vision.md`, and syncs stage/codex changes
279	through the typed cartograph API. Edits to the writ are **not**
280	propagated back to the file — the data flow is one-way (file → writ).
281	
282	### Stale-binding recovery
283	
284	If the bound writ has been deleted, cancelled, completed, or failed,
285	the next apply errors cleanly with no partial writes. To recover,
286	either restore the writ to a non-terminal state, or clear the
287	`visionId` field in the sidecar to let the next apply re-create the
288	vision (the orphaned writ stays in place; `nsg writ cancel` it
289	manually if needed).
290	
291	### Priority-hint semantics (`ext['surveyor']`)
292	
293	Each apply writes a payload into `writ.ext['surveyor']` carrying the
294	fields enumerated above (`severity`, `deadline`, `decay`,
295	`complexity`). CLI flags override sidecar values for the merged
296	result:
297	
298	```sh
299	# sidecar severity=low; CLI override wins
300	nsg vision apply my-vision --severity high
301	```
302	
303	Fields that neither source provides are omitted from the payload. The
304	slot is **always** written — even when the merged payload is `{}` — so
305	its presence on the writ marks it as having been processed by the
306	apply tool.
307	
308	The slot's owner — a future surveyor-apparatus plugin that consumes
309	these hints — does not yet ship. Until it does, the slot is inert; the
310	key (`surveyor`) and the payload shape are load-bearing precedent for
311	that future commission.
312	
313	## What is *not* in this commission
314	
315	- No vision-keeper agent runtime. `vision-keeper.md` is a placeholder
316	  stub; the agent runtime ships in a separate commission.
317	- No per-type tree command. `nsg writ tree --type vision` already
318	  renders the writ-level shape; the cartograph-aware tree renderer
319	  (with stage badges per row) is a future commission.
320	- No pages — the Oculus writs page auto-renders the new types via its
321	  type-vocabulary helper.
322	- No link kinds — parent edges flow through `writ.parentId`. Typed link
323	  kinds are deferred.
324	- No `childrenBehavior` cascade — patron-walkthrough semantics are
325	  coordinated by the typed API and downstream consumers.
326

file.content:
# `@shardworks/cartograph-apparatus`

The Cartograph stands up the **decomposition-ladder** substrate: a typed
data layer for tracking long-lived patron intent across four levels —
**vision** (top, patron-owned, long-lived) → **charge** (first
decomposition, the unit of patron walkthrough) → **piece** (recursive,
internal organization, self-nesting) → **mandate** (the existing leaf
where rigs attach).

This commission lands the **data-and-typed-API substrate plus a
patron-facing CLI**. There is no agent runtime and no downstream
consumer in this package — those land in follow-on commissions. What
ships here:

- Three new writ types contributed to the Clerk: `vision`, `charge`,
  `piece`. Each uses a six-state mandate-clone lifecycle (no
  `childrenBehavior` cascade — patron-walkthrough semantics will be
  coordinated by the typed API and downstream consumers, not by
  registry-side cascade rules).
- Three companion books (`visions`, `charges`, `pieces`) keyed by the
  writ id, each holding a typed companion document with the per-type
  stage and audit fields.
- A `CartographApi` exposed via `provides` that is the **only** layer
  enforcing the ladder's parent invariants:
  - vision has no parent;
  - `charge.parentId` must be a vision;
  - `piece.parentId` must be a charge or piece.
  Raw `clerk.post({ type: 'vision' })` continues to succeed without
  parent-type checks — the typed API is the validator. The mandate-side
  rules (mandate may attach under any non-terminal node) stay where they
  are.
- A patron-facing CLI surface contributed via `supportKit.tools` —
  three subcommand groups (`nsg vision`, `nsg charge`, `nsg piece`),
  each with five operations (`create`, `show`, `list`, `patch`,
  `transition`) plus the on-disk authoring tool `nsg vision apply`.
  The framework `nsg` auto-builder discovers the tools at startup and
  groups them by hyphen prefix; no edits to the framework CLI package
  are required. Every tool routes through the typed API above, so the
  parent invariants and lifecycle coupling hold for CLI-driven
  authoring.

The Cartograph requires `stacks` and `clerk` and recommends `oculus`. The
Oculus writs page automatically renders the new types via the
type-vocabulary helper; without oculus the data is invisible to the
dashboard.

---

## Installation

```json
{
  "dependencies": {
    "@shardworks/cartograph-apparatus": "workspace:*"
  }
}
```

## API

The Cartograph exposes a `CartographApi` via `provides`, accessible at
runtime via `guild().apparatus<CartographApi>('cartograph')`.

```typescript
import type { CartographApi } from '@shardworks/cartograph-apparatus';

const cartograph = guild().apparatus<CartographApi>('cartograph');

// Create a vision (no parent permitted)
const vision = await cartograph.createVision({
  title: 'Land the agentic decomposition ladder',
  body: 'Long-form patron intent ...',
  codex: 'main',
});

// Create a charge under that vision
const charge = await cartograph.createCharge({
  parentId: vision.id,
  title: 'Stand up the data substrate',
  body: 'First decomposition ...',
});

// Create a piece under the charge
const piece = await cartograph.createPiece({
  parentId: charge.id,
  title: 'Pick a companion-doc shape',
  body: 'Internal organization ...',
});

// Pieces can self-nest
const subPiece = await cartograph.createPiece({
  parentId: piece.id,
  title: 'Decide how createX writes both rows atomically',
  body: 'Internal sub-piece ...',
});
```

Each `createX` opens a single `stacks.transaction(...)` and writes the
writ row and the companion doc inside one atomic boundary. Parent
existence, parent-not-terminal, codex inheritance, and id generation
match Clerk's `post()` validation byte-for-byte (the duplication is the
cost of being a typed atomic surface).

### `CartographApi`

```typescript
interface CartographApi {
  // Vision
  createVision(req: CreateVisionRequest): Promise<VisionDoc>;
  showVision(id: string): Promise<VisionDoc>;
  listVisions(filters?: VisionFilters): Promise<VisionDoc[]>;
  patchVision(id: string, fields: Partial<Omit<VisionDoc, 'id'>>): Promise<VisionDoc>;
  transitionVision(id: string, to: { phase: WritPhase; stage: VisionStage; resolution?: string }): Promise<VisionDoc>;

  // Charge
  createCharge(req: CreateChargeRequest): Promise<ChargeDoc>;
  showCharge(id: string): Promise<ChargeDoc>;
  listCharges(filters?: ChargeFilters): Promise<ChargeDoc[]>;
  patchCharge(id: string, fields: Partial<Omit<ChargeDoc, 'id'>>): Promise<ChargeDoc>;
  transitionCharge(id: string, to: { phase: WritPhase; stage: ChargeStage; resolution?: string }): Promise<ChargeDoc>;

  // Piece
  createPiece(req: CreatePieceRequest): Promise<PieceDoc>;
  showPiece(id: string): Promise<PieceDoc>;
  listPieces(filters?: PieceFilters): Promise<PieceDoc[]>;
  patchPiece(id: string, fields: Partial<Omit<PieceDoc, 'id'>>): Promise<PieceDoc>;
  transitionPiece(id: string, to: { phase: WritPhase; stage: PieceStage; resolution?: string }): Promise<PieceDoc>;
}
```

The lifecycle-coupled `transitionX` methods update both `writ.phase` and
the companion doc's `stage` field atomically, inside a single Stacks
transaction. The caller specifies both the target phase and the target
stage explicitly because a single phase may map to multiple stages
depending on context (e.g. a charge moving to `failed` could mean
stage `dropped` or stage `validated` depending on outcome).

### Companion documents

```typescript
interface VisionDoc { id: string; stage: VisionStage; codex?: string; createdAt: string; updatedAt: string; }
interface ChargeDoc { id: string; stage: ChargeStage; codex?: string; createdAt: string; updatedAt: string; }
interface PieceDoc  { id: string; stage: PieceStage;  codex?: string; createdAt: string; updatedAt: string; }

type VisionStage = 'draft' | 'active' | 'sunset' | 'cancelled';
type ChargeStage = 'draft' | 'active' | 'validated' | 'dropped';
type PieceStage  = 'draft' | 'active' | 'done' | 'dropped';
```

The minimal field set is deliberate — the patch surface plus the
`[key: string]: unknown` index signature lets consumers grow the field
set non-breakingly later. Vision text lives on `writ.body`; the
companion doc carries typed metadata only.

### Filters

`VisionFilters`, `ChargeFilters`, and `PieceFilters` mirror the
`PlanFilters` shape from astrolabe — `{ stage?, codex?, limit?, offset? }`.
Lists are ordered by `createdAt desc` (newest first).

## Support Kit

### Books

| Book | Owner | Indexes |
|---|---|---|
| `visions` | `cartograph` | `stage`, `codex`, `createdAt` |
| `charges` | `cartograph` | `stage`, `codex`, `createdAt` |
| `pieces`  | `cartograph` | `stage`, `codex`, `createdAt` |

### Writ Types (contributed to Clerk)

| Name | Description |
|---|---|
| `vision` | Top-level patron intent. No parent. Long-lived. |
| `charge` | First decomposition under a vision; the unit of patron walkthrough. Parent must be a vision. |
| `piece`  | Recursive internal organization. Self-nests. Parent must be a charge or piece. |

Each writ type uses a six-state mandate-clone lifecycle:
`new` (initial) → `open` (active) → `stuck`/`completed`/`failed`/`cancelled`;
no `childrenBehavior` cascade.

## CLI

Cartograph contributes three subcommand groups to `nsg` — one per writ
type — with the same five operations under each:

| Command | Description |
|---|---|
| `nsg vision create --title <t> --body <b> [--codex <c>]` | Create a top-level vision (writ at `phase: new`, doc at `stage: draft`). |
| `nsg charge create --parent-id <vision> --title <t> --body <b> [--codex <c>]` | Create a charge under a vision. |
| `nsg piece create --parent-id <charge\|piece> --title <t> --body <b> [--codex <c>]` | Create a piece under a charge or piece (self-nests). |
| `nsg <type> show <id> [--format text\|json]` | Show the companion doc joined with the writ row. Text mode mirrors `nsg writ show`'s lifecycle-aware block; JSON returns `{ ...doc, writ: { ... } }`. |
| `nsg <type> list [--stage <s>] [--codex <c>] [--limit <n>] [--offset <n>] [--format text\|json]` | Tabular list (STAGE \| ID \| CODEX \| TITLE \| CREATED) ordered by `createdAt desc`. |
| `nsg <type> patch <id> --codex <c>` | Patch the companion doc's `codex`. Title and body live on the writ row — edit them via `nsg writ edit`. |
| `nsg <type> transition <id> --phase <writ-phase> --stage <doc-stage> [--resolution <r>]` | Atomically advance both `writ.phase` and the companion doc's `stage` inside one Stacks transaction. Both `--phase` and `--stage` are required because a single phase may map to multiple stages depending on context. |
| `nsg vision apply <slug> [--severity <s>] [--deadline <d>] [--decay <d>]` | Snapshot the on-disk vision at `<GUILD>/vision/<slug>/` into a vision writ + VisionDoc. Idempotent: first apply creates and binds; later applies sync the file's contents into the bound writ. CLI flags override sidecar values for the surveyor priority-hint payload. |

The CLI tools are read or write according to operation: `show`/`list`
declare `permission: 'read'`; `create`/`patch`/`transition`/`apply`
declare `permission: 'write'`. Every tool declares
`callableBy: ['patron']` — they are not exposed to anima or library
callers in this commission. Only `show` and `list` accept the
`--format text|json` flag; write tools return their result directly
and the framework auto-stringifies.

Short-prefix id resolution (via `clerk.resolveId`) works on every
id-bearing flag: `nsg vision show w-mo123` and
`nsg charge create --parent-id w-mo123` both succeed when the prefix
matches a single writ.

## On-disk vision authoring

Visions are prose-heavy and long-lived; the patron-facing path is to
author them in a normal editor on disk and snapshot the directory into
the cartograph with `nsg vision apply <slug>`. The same code path
handles first-time creation and Nth re-import — the binding is durable
across applies, so the file tree is the source of truth for vision
content while the cartograph remains the source of truth for lifecycle
state and ext metadata.

### File layout

```
<GUILD>/
└── vision/
    └── <slug>/
        ├── vision.md              # long-form vision text → writ.body
        └── vision-metadata.yml    # required sidecar
```

The slug must be a single directory name (lowercase letters, digits,
hyphens, or underscores; no path separators, no leading dots, no
`..`). It is fixed at creation time; rename is not supported.

### Sidecar shape

```yaml
# vision-metadata.yml
title: Land the agentic decomposition ladder    # required
stage: draft                                    # required: draft | active

codex: main                                     # optional
severity: high                                  # optional priority hint
deadline: 2026-06-30                            # optional priority hint
decay: slow                                     # optional priority hint
complexity: medium                              # optional priority hint (sidecar-only)
resolution: patron retired the vision           # optional, used on terminal transitions

# Managed by `nsg vision apply` — do not edit by hand.
visionId: w-...                                 # written after first apply
```

| Field | Required | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `title` | yes | Short human-readable title for the vision writ. |
| `stage` | yes | Lifecycle stage. Allowed initial values are `draft` (writ phase `new`) and `active` (writ phase `open`). `sunset` and `cancelled` are rejected on first apply — a vision cannot be born retired; transition to a terminal stage on a later apply. |
| `codex` | no | Target codex name for the vision. |
| `visionId` | no, system-managed | Written by `apply` after first creation. **Do not edit by hand.** Clear the field only to deliberately re-create the vision (the old writ stays in place and becomes orphaned from this directory). |
| `severity` | no | Priority hint surfaced in `writ.ext['surveyor']`. |
| `deadline` | no | Priority hint surfaced in `writ.ext['surveyor']`. |
| `decay` | no | Priority hint surfaced in `writ.ext['surveyor']`. |
| `complexity` | no | Priority hint surfaced in `writ.ext['surveyor']`. Sidecar-only — there is no CLI flag for this field. |
| `resolution` | no | Passed through to `transitionVision` on terminal moves. |

Unknown keys are logged as a warning and ignored (preserves
forward-compatibility while still surfacing typos).

### `visionId` binding

After the first successful `nsg vision apply <slug>`, the tool writes
the new writ's id back into the sidecar as `visionId`. The yaml is
round-tripped via `parseDocument`, so comments and key order survive
the rewrite, and the file is replaced atomically (write-temp,
rename-over) so a process crash cannot lose the binding.

On every subsequent apply the tool resolves the writ via `visionId`,
updates `writ.body` from `vision.md`, and syncs stage/codex changes
through the typed cartograph API. Edits to the writ are **not**
propagated back to the file — the data flow is one-way (file → writ).

### Stale-binding recovery

If the bound writ has been deleted, cancelled, completed, or failed,
the next apply errors cleanly with no partial writes. To recover,
either restore the writ to a non-terminal state, or clear the
`visionId` field in the sidecar to let the next apply re-create the
vision (the orphaned writ stays in place; `nsg writ cancel` it
manually if needed).

### Priority-hint semantics (`ext['surveyor']`)

Each apply writes a payload into `writ.ext['surveyor']` carrying the
fields enumerated above (`severity`, `deadline`, `decay`,
`complexity`). CLI flags override sidecar values for the merged
result:

```sh
# sidecar severity=low; CLI override wins
nsg vision apply my-vision --severity high
```

Fields that neither source provides are omitted from the payload. The
slot is **always** written — even when the merged payload is `{}` — so
its presence on the writ marks it as having been processed by the
apply tool.

The slot's owner — a future surveyor-apparatus plugin that consumes
these hints — does not yet ship. Until it does, the slot is inert; the
key (`surveyor`) and the payload shape are load-bearing precedent for
that future commission.

## What is *not* in this commission

- No vision-keeper agent runtime. `vision-keeper.md` is a placeholder
  stub; the agent runtime ships in a separate commission.
- No per-type tree command. `nsg writ tree --type vision` already
  renders the writ-level shape; the cartograph-aware tree renderer
  (with stage badges per row) is a future commission.
- No pages — the Oculus writs page auto-renders the new types via its
  type-vocabulary helper.
- No link kinds — parent edges flow through `writ.parentId`. Typed link
  kinds are deferred.
- No `childrenBehavior` cascade — patron-walkthrough semantics are
  coordinated by the typed API and downstream consumers.

1	import { z } from 'zod';
2	import { guild } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
3	import { tool } from '@shardworks/tools-apparatus';
4	import type { CartographApi } from '../types.ts';
5	import type { ClerkApi } from '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus';
6	
7	/**
8	 * Allowed writ phases across all writ types (mirrors `WritPhase` from
9	 * the Clerk API). Required at the Zod boundary so `--help` enumerates
10	 * the legal values (D16). The typed API still enforces per-state
11	 * `allowedTransitions` and rejects illegal edges.
12	 */
13	const WRIT_PHASES = ['new', 'open', 'stuck', 'completed', 'failed', 'cancelled'] as const;
14	
15	/**
16	 * Vision-specific stage enum (mirrors `VisionStage` from `../types.ts`).
17	 * Per D15 the caller supplies both phase and stage explicitly because a
18	 * single phase may map to multiple stages depending on context.
19	 */
20	const VISION_STAGES = ['draft', 'active', 'sunset', 'cancelled'] as const;
21	
22	/**
23	 * Transition a vision: writes both `writ.phase` and the companion doc's
24	 * `stage` atomically inside one Stacks transaction. Both `--phase` and
25	 * `--stage` are required (D15) and Zod-enum-validated (D16). Optional
26	 * `--resolution` lands in the same transaction (D17).
27	 */
28	export default tool({
29	  name: 'vision-transition',
30	  description: 'Transition a vision lifecycle (writes phase + stage atomically)',
31	  instructions:
32	    'Atomically updates the vision writ\'s phase and the companion doc\'s stage. ' +
33	    'Both --phase and --stage are required: a single phase may map to multiple ' +
34	    'stages depending on context (e.g. failed → cancelled vs failed → sunset). ' +
35	    'The typed API rejects illegal phase edges per the writ-type config.',
36	  params: {
37	    id: z.string().describe('Vision id (or short prefix)'),
38	    phase: z.enum(WRIT_PHASES).describe('Target phase on the underlying writ'),
39	    stage: z.enum(VISION_STAGES).describe('Target stage on the companion vision doc'),
40	    resolution: z
41	      .string()
42	      .optional()
43	      .describe('Optional resolution string. Recorded on terminal transitions.'),
44	  },
45	  permission: 'write',
46	  callableBy: ['patron'],
47	  handler: async (params) => {
48	    const cartograph = guild().apparatus<CartographApi>('cartograph');
49	    const clerk = guild().apparatus<ClerkApi>('clerk');
50	    const resolvedId = await clerk.resolveId(params.id);
51	    return cartograph.transitionVision(resolvedId, {
52	      phase: params.phase,
53	      stage: params.stage,
54	      ...(params.resolution !== undefined ? { resolution: params.resolution } : {}),
55	    });
56	  },
57	});
58

144:   * Controls when the handler runs relative to the transaction commit.
146:   * true  (default) — Phase 1: runs INSIDE the transaction. Handler writes
147:   *   join the same transaction. If the handler throws, everything rolls back.
149:   * false — Phase 2: runs AFTER the transaction commits. Errors are logged
175:  transaction<R>(fn: (tx: TransactionContext) => Promise<R>): Promise<R>;

1	/**
2	 * `reckoner.tick` — periodic tick relay.
3	 *
4	 * Wired into the apparatus's `supportKit.relays` so the Clockworks
5	 * dispatches every fire of the Reckoner's standing order
6	 * (`@every 60s` → `reckoner.tick`) into this handler. The standing
7	 * order itself ships from `apparatus.supportKit.standingOrders`; no
8	 * operator action is required to enable the tick.
9	 *
10	 * Per-fire sequence (the canonical evaluation entry):
11	 *
12	 *   1. Resolve the active scheduler. If the scheduler reference is not
13	 *      yet populated (`phase:started` has not fired), throw fail-loud.
14	 *      Production never trips this — the framework gates `start()` on
15	 *      `requires: ['clerk']` and seals the registry on `phase:started`,
16	 *      both before the Clockworks could possibly schedule a tick — but
17	 *      tests can assert the throw, and a silent skip would hide
18	 *      ordering drift.
19	 *
20	 *   2. Query held petitions (writs in `new` phase carrying
21	 *      `ext.reckoner`) by reading `clerk/writs` directly,
22	 *      deliberately literal-phase-only — the type-agnostic
23	 *      generalization (iterating Clerk's writ-type registry for
24	 *      non-`'new'` initial phases) is observed and lifted as a
25	 *      separate concern.
26	 *
27	 *   3. Apply source / disabled-source gates inline. Each writ that
28	 *      fails a gate produces a `declined` Reckonings row and a
29	 *      transition to its cancelled-target phase, then is filtered out
30	 *      of the candidate set.
31	 *
32	 *   4. Filter the candidate set against the
33	 *      `(writId, writUpdatedAt)` dedupe lookup before building
34	 *      `SchedulerInput`. Already-considered writs never reach the
35	 *      scheduler. Deferred rows do not count as "already
36	 *      considered" — the dependency-aware defer path writes
37	 *      state-change audit entries that must not gate next-tick
38	 *      re-evaluation.
39	 *
40	 *   5. Run the dependency-aware consideration gate (D1–D13 of the
41	 *      dependency-aware-consideration commission) on each surviving
42	 *      candidate. A writ with one or more outbound `depends-on`
43	 *      links whose targets are not all `cleared` produces a
44	 *      deferred Reckonings row (with no-op-row suppression on the
45	 *      same outcome shape) and is dropped from the candidate set.
46	 *      Failed-precedence aggregation chooses between
47	 *      `dependency_pending` and `dependency_failed`.
48	 *
49	 *   6. Re-read and validate `reckoner.schedulerConfig`. On a
50	 *      `validateConfig` throw, log fail-loud and skip the entire
51	 *      tick.
52	 *
53	 *   7. Build a single `SchedulerInput { candidates, capacity, now,
54	 *      config }` (capacity is the v0 stub) and call `evaluate`. On a
55	 *      throw or non-array return, log fail-loud and skip the tick.
56	 *
57	 *   8. Validate decisions. Filter-and-warn on stranger writIds (apply
58	 *      only in-scope decisions). Fail-loud-skip the entire tick on
59	 *      multi-decision-per-writ.
60	 *
61	 *   9. Apply decisions. `approve` → transition to active target via
62	 *      `resolveActiveTargetPhase`; `decline` → transition to
63	 *      cancelled with the decision's reason as the resolution
64	 *      string; `defer` → no transition. Append one Reckonings row
65	 *      per writ considered. `defer` rows carry

file.content:
/**
 * `reckoner.tick` — periodic tick relay.
 *
 * Wired into the apparatus's `supportKit.relays` so the Clockworks
 * dispatches every fire of the Reckoner's standing order
 * (`@every 60s` → `reckoner.tick`) into this handler. The standing
 * order itself ships from `apparatus.supportKit.standingOrders`; no
 * operator action is required to enable the tick.
 *
 * Per-fire sequence (the canonical evaluation entry):
 *
 *   1. Resolve the active scheduler. If the scheduler reference is not
 *      yet populated (`phase:started` has not fired), throw fail-loud.
 *      Production never trips this — the framework gates `start()` on
 *      `requires: ['clerk']` and seals the registry on `phase:started`,
 *      both before the Clockworks could possibly schedule a tick — but
 *      tests can assert the throw, and a silent skip would hide
 *      ordering drift.
 *
 *   2. Query held petitions (writs in `new` phase carrying
 *      `ext.reckoner`) by reading `clerk/writs` directly,
 *      deliberately literal-phase-only — the type-agnostic
 *      generalization (iterating Clerk's writ-type registry for
 *      non-`'new'` initial phases) is observed and lifted as a
 *      separate concern.
 *
 *   3. Apply source / disabled-source gates inline. Each writ that
 *      fails a gate produces a `declined` Reckonings row and a
 *      transition to its cancelled-target phase, then is filtered out
 *      of the candidate set.
 *
 *   4. Filter the candidate set against the
 *      `(writId, writUpdatedAt)` dedupe lookup before building
 *      `SchedulerInput`. Already-considered writs never reach the
 *      scheduler. Deferred rows do not count as "already
 *      considered" — the dependency-aware defer path writes
 *      state-change audit entries that must not gate next-tick
 *      re-evaluation.
 *
 *   5. Run the dependency-aware consideration gate (D1–D13 of the
 *      dependency-aware-consideration commission) on each surviving
 *      candidate. A writ with one or more outbound `depends-on`
 *      links whose targets are not all `cleared` produces a
 *      deferred Reckonings row (with no-op-row suppression on the
 *      same outcome shape) and is dropped from the candidate set.
 *      Failed-precedence aggregation chooses between
 *      `dependency_pending` and `dependency_failed`.
 *
 *   6. Re-read and validate `reckoner.schedulerConfig`. On a
 *      `validateConfig` throw, log fail-loud and skip the entire
 *      tick.
 *
 *   7. Build a single `SchedulerInput { candidates, capacity, now,
 *      config }` (capacity is the v0 stub) and call `evaluate`. On a
 *      throw or non-array return, log fail-loud and skip the tick.
 *
 *   8. Validate decisions. Filter-and-warn on stranger writIds (apply
 *      only in-scope decisions). Fail-loud-skip the entire tick on
 *      multi-decision-per-writ.
 *
 *   9. Apply decisions. `approve` → transition to active target via
 *      `resolveActiveTargetPhase`; `decline` → transition to
 *      cancelled with the decision's reason as the resolution
 *      string; `defer` → no transition. Append one Reckonings row
 *      per writ considered. `defer` rows carry
 *      `deferReason: 'other'` and the decision's reason in
 *      `deferNote`.
 *
 *  10. Stamp `tickEventId` on every row from the triggering
 *      `clockworks.timer` event id when present; omit the field when
 *      absent (e.g. test paths driving the handler with `null`).
 *
 * Source-file layout follows the established stdlib-relay pattern
 * (`packages/plugins/clockworks/src/summon-relay.ts`). The relay
 * factory and the pure handler-body helper are co-located here so
 * unit tests can drive the helper without booting Clockworks.
 *
 * See: docs/architecture/apparatus/reckoner.md (the apparatus shape),
 * docs/architecture/reckonings-book.md (the Reckonings book schema
 * and tick-event contract).
 */

import type { GuildEvent, RelayDefinition } from '@shardworks/clockworks-apparatus';
import { relay } from '@shardworks/clockworks-apparatus';
import type {
  Book,
  ReadOnlyBook,
  StacksApi,
} from '@shardworks/stacks-apparatus';
import type { ClerkApi, WritDoc, WritPhase } from '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus';

import type {
  ReckoningDoc,
  ReckonerExt,
  Scheduler,
  SchedulerDecision,
} from './types.ts';

/** Plugin id stamped on `writ.ext['reckoner']`. Hardcoded literal — same constant the apparatus core uses. */
const RECKONER_PLUGIN_ID = 'reckoner';

/** Relay name registered with the Clockworks. Matches the standing-order `run:` field byte-for-byte. */
export const RECKONER_TICK_RELAY_NAME = 'reckoner.tick';

/** Schedule string the Reckoner contributes via `apparatus.supportKit.standingOrders`. */
export const RECKONER_TICK_SCHEDULE = '@every 60s';

// ── Tick handler context ───────────────────────────────────────────

 * The dependency context the tick handler closes over. The factory
 * inside `buildReckoner()` constructs this from its own closure-scoped
 * state (the registry maps, the resolved active scheduler, the
 * Reckonings book handle, the config / scheduler-config resolvers,
 * the type-aware target-phase resolver, the dedupe lookup, the row
 * builder) and threads it through the relay factory.
 *
 * The same shape is consumed by `runTickHandler` so unit tests can
 * drive the per-fire sequence without booting Clockworks.
 */
export interface TickContext {
  /** Clerk handle. Used for `transition` and `getWritTypeConfig`. */
  clerk: ClerkApi;

  reckoningsBook: Book<ReckoningDoc>;

   * `undefined` until `phase:started` resolves the selector.
   */
  getActiveScheduler: () => Scheduler | undefined;
  /** Resolves the live source / disabled-source config slice. */
  resolveConfig: () => ResolvedReckonerConfig;
  /** Re-reads `reckoner.schedulerConfig` from `guild.json`. */
  resolveSchedulerConfig: () => unknown;
  /** Returns `true` when `source` is in the petitioner registry. */
  isSourceRegistered: (source: string) => boolean;
  /**
   * Resolves the type-aware active target phase for a writ. Throws
   * fail-loud on ambiguity or on a missing/unconfigured type.
   */
  resolveActiveTargetPhase: (writ: WritDoc) => string;
  /**
   * Dedupe lookup. Returns `true` when a Reckonings row already
   * exists for the (writId, writUpdatedAt) pair. Deferred rows do
   * not count — the dependency-aware defer path writes deferred
   * rows as state-change audit entries while leaving the writ in
   * `new` phase, and subsequent ticks must remain free to re-run
   * the gate at the same `writUpdatedAt`.
   */
  alreadyConsidered: (writId: string, writUpdatedAt: string) => Promise<boolean>;
  /** Build a Reckonings row from in-flight consideration state. */
  buildReckoningRow: (params: BuildReckoningRowParams) => ReckoningDoc;
  /**
   * Run the dependency-aware consideration gate against a single
   * held petition under tick consideration (D1–D13 of the
   * dependency-aware-consideration commission). Returns
   * `proceed: true` when the writ should remain in the candidate
   * set for the scheduler; `proceed: false` when the writ has been
   * deferred (a deferred Reckonings row may have been written, with
   * no-op-row suppression applied) and should be dropped from the
   * candidate set. The dep gate runs every tick — outside the
   * `(writId, writUpdatedAt)` dedupe short-circuit — so a writ
   * deferred on dependencies keeps being re-evaluated as those
   * dependencies clear.
   */
  runDependencyGate: (args: {
    writ: WritDoc;
    ext: ReckonerExt;

content.content:
69:The guild's operational fabric is provided by apparatus — plugins with a start/stop lifecycle that Arbor starts in dependency order. **The Stacks** is the persistence substrate everything else reads from and writes to. **The Scriptorium** manages codexes — bare clones, draft bindings (worktrees), and the seal-and-push lifecycle. **The Clockworks** is the event-driven nervous system: standing orders bind events to relays, and the summon relay dispatches anima sessions in response. **The Surveyor** is the cartograph-decomposition substrate: it surveys cartograph nodes (visions, charges, pieces), produces structural decompositions, and routes each node to whichever kit-contributed surveyor claims its node-type — see [Surveying Cascade](surveying-cascade.md) for the contract. **The Clerk** handles commission intake, converting patron requests into writs and signaling when work is ready to execute. The Fabricator, Spider, Executor, Loom, and Animator then take it from there — covered in the next section.
286:The Surveyor is anticipated as the planned `surveyor` package — the cartograph-decomposition substrate described in [Surveying Cascade](surveying-cascade.md), landing in a separate commission. The Executor is described elsewhere in this document as part of the guild's operational fabric, but is not yet extracted as a standalone package.

content.content:
183:The apparatus that surveys the cartograph — the guild's structural map of visions, charges, and pieces. When a node lands on the cartograph, the Surveyor decomposes it: walking its substance, breaking it into the structural pieces an animator or sage can act on, and recording what was found alongside the node. The Surveyor does not judge what to build; it produces the structural inventory that downstream apparatus and animas reason against.
185:The Surveyor is itself a substrate — a kit-contributable surveyor registry. Each kit ships its own surveyor for the cartograph node-types it understands; the substrate routes a node to whichever surveyor claims it and threads the resulting decomposition back into the cartograph. Without a current survey, the guild has structure on the cartograph but no idea what is inside it.
187:The surveyor's decompositions live alongside the cartograph in the guildhall — the cartograph holds the structure, the surveyor's record holds what was found inside each piece. See [The Surveying Cascade](architecture/surveying-cascade.md) for the substrate's contract.

docs/guild-metaphor.md:185:The Surveyor is itself a substrate — a kit-contributable surveyor registry. Each kit ships its own surveyor for the cartograph node-types it understands; the substrate routes a node to whichever surveyor claims it and threads the resulting decomposition back into the cartograph. Without a current survey, the guild has structure on the cartograph but no idea what is inside it.
docs/guild-metaphor.md:187:The surveyor's decompositions live alongside the cartograph in the guildhall — the cartograph holds the structure, the surveyor's record holds what was found inside each piece. See [The Surveying Cascade](architecture/surveying-cascade.md) for the substrate's contract.
docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md:46:| **`ext['surveyor']`** | Plugin-keyed `writ.ext` slot owned by the surveyor-apparatus. Carries priority hints (severity, deadline, decay, complexity) on cartograph nodes — set by the patron via apply CLI for visions, set by the upstream surveyor rig for charges and pieces. The substrate translates these into Reckoner dimensions when emitting the survey petition. |
docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md:87:  │ creates child charges via cartograph API + sets ext['surveyor'] hints
docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md:348:The cartograph's existing `show`/`list` tools serve surveyor anima
docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md:601:- Each cartograph node's `ext['surveyor']` records its priority intent
docs/architecture/index.md:69:The guild's operational fabric is provided by apparatus — plugins with a start/stop lifecycle that Arbor starts in dependency order. **The Stacks** is the persistence substrate everything else reads from and writes to. **The Scriptorium** manages codexes — bare clones, draft bindings (worktrees), and the seal-and-push lifecycle. **The Clockworks** is the event-driven nervous system: standing orders bind events to relays, and the summon relay dispatches anima sessions in response. **The Surveyor** is the cartograph-decomposition substrate: it surveys cartograph nodes (visions, charges, pieces), produces structural decompositions, and routes each node to whichever kit-contributed surveyor claims its node-type — see [Surveying Cascade](surveying-cascade.md) for the contract. **The Clerk** handles commission intake, converting patron requests into writs and signaling when work is ready to execute. The Fabricator, Spider, Executor, Loom, and Animator then take it from there — covered in the next section.
docs/architecture/index.md:286:The Surveyor is anticipated as the planned `surveyor` package — the cartograph-decomposition substrate described in [Surveying Cascade](surveying-cascade.md), landing in a separate commission. The Executor is described elsewhere in this document as part of the guild's operational fabric, but is not yet extracted as a standalone package.

content.text: Now I have enough understanding to write the plan artifacts. Let me write them.

scope.description: Migrate cartograph storage from three companion books to writ.ext['cartograph'] = { stage, codex }, removing the books and rebuilding the typed-API internals on clerk.post + clerk.setWritExt + clerk.transition while keeping the CartographApi surface byte-for-byte stable. Includes companion-book deletion, projection-from-ext logic for showX/listX, atomicity wrapping per brief §3, supportKit.books removal, and updates to cartograph.test.ts / tools.test.ts / vision-apply.test.ts (CDC counters migrate from cartograph/visions watch to writs-book watch filtered by entry.type, fixture stops pre-creating the cartograph books, supportKit.books assertion is rewritten). README updates on the cartograph package go here as concurrent doc updates.
scope.rationale: This is the entirety of the storage-substrate migration the brief commissions; the typed-API stability and the test/doc updates are inseparable from the data-layer change.

scope.description: Update the surveying-cascade architecture document at docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md — specifically §3.4 (Companion SurveyDoc), §3.6 (Substrate watches cartograph CDC), §3.7 (Substrate plugin shape), plus minor incidental references elsewhere in the doc (worked-example mermaid CDC arrows, etc.) — to reflect the post-cleanup shape so Commission C is briefed against it.
scope.rationale: Brief §6 explicitly schedules these three sections for rewrite; brief Motivation §2 names the alignment as a downstream-substrate prerequisite. Could in principle be deferred to Commission C, but the patron has named it as part of this commission's deliverable.

decisions.question: How does cartograph achieve atomicity when writing the writ row + ext['cartograph'] sub-slot (createX) and the writ.phase + ext['cartograph'].stage pair (transitionX)?
decisions.context: clerk.transition silently strips ext from its body (clerk.ts:1146-1149); the only sanctioned ext-write path is clerk.setWritExt, which opens its own transaction internally. stacks.transaction calls flatten when nested (stacks-core.ts:191-196), so a single outer wrapper makes the two distinct clerk-API calls join one transaction.

options.wrapInOuterTransaction: Each createX / transitionX opens a single stacks.transaction(...). Inside, call clerk.post (or clerk.transition) followed by clerk.setWritExt. Both join the outer tx via flattening; one CDC update event coalesces from the pair.
options.extendClerkApi: Extend ClerkApi with a new transition-with-ext primitive that bundles transition + setWritExt server-side.
options.inlineRawWrites: Skip the clerk API and write directly to the writs book via tx.book<WritDoc>('clerk','writs').put / patch, computing the new ext shape inline.

decisions.rationale: Brief §3 prescribes this exact shape; flattening primitive already exists; option B extends the API at the wrong layer (per Three Defaults: extend the API at the right layer, but the brief explicitly carves this out as cartograph-side wrapping); option C bypasses clerk's validation byte-for-byte.

decisions.question: Should createX delegate to clerk.post() for the writ-row creation, or write directly to the writs book inside the transaction (mirroring today's code)?
decisions.context: Today's createVision opens a stacks.transaction and writes the writ row directly via tx.book<WritDoc>('clerk','writs').put — explicitly NOT delegating to clerk.post because clerk.post does not accept an external transaction context. The cartograph.ts comment at lines 303-307 documents this: 'The createX methods cannot delegate to clerk.post because Clerk's post does not accept an external transaction context, and the writ row + companion doc must commit under one boundary.' Post-cleanup, writes still need to commit atomically, but clerk.post would need to be called from inside the outer transaction.

options.keepDirectStacksWrite: Continue writing the writ row directly via tx.book<WritDoc>('clerk','writs').put inside cartograph's transaction. Replicate clerk.post's id generation, codex inheritance, parent validation. Write ext['cartograph'] via tx.book + manual ext merge OR call clerk.setWritExt (joins outer tx).
options.delegateToClerkPost: Inside cartograph's outer stacks.transaction, call clerk.post(...) and then clerk.setWritExt(...). Both nested transactions flatten into the outer one. Clerk's post handles id generation + parent validation + codex inheritance natively.
options.extendClerkPost: Extend ClerkApi.post to accept an optional ext argument so the writ row + ext sub-slot are written in one clerk-side transaction.

decisions.rationale: The original code's comment about not being able to delegate is now obsolete — stacks.transaction nesting flattens, so clerk.post's internal transaction joins cartograph's outer wrapper. Eliminates 100+ lines of duplicated parent-validation / codex-inheritance / id-generation logic. Three Defaults: extend the API at the right layer is option C, but the brief explicitly says 'Reworking the ext API itself' is out of scope and clerk.post extension would arguably qualify as ext-API rework. Option A keeps existing duplication and is harder to reason about.

decisions.question: How does showX / listX read the projection — query the writs book directly via clerk.list, query via stacks.readBook<WritDoc>, or via clerk.show?
decisions.context: The doc projection needs the writ row + writ.ext['cartograph']. Three obvious primitives: clerk.show(id) (single-row), clerk.list({ type: 'vision' }) (typed list), or stacks.readBook<WritDoc>('clerk','writs').find(...). Reckoner uses stacks.readBook directly to bypass clerk.list's mandate-implicit filter; cartograph's tools/render.ts already calls clerk.show(id) for the show composer.

options.useClerkApi: showVision → clerk.show(id) + project. listVisions → clerk.list({ type: 'vision', ... }) + project + apply ext.cartograph filter on top.
options.useStacksDirectly: showVision → stacks.readBook<WritDoc>('clerk','writs').get(id) + project. listVisions → readBook.find({ where: [['type','=','vision'], ['ext.cartograph.stage','=',stage]], orderBy: ['createdAt','desc'] }).
options.mixed: showVision via clerk.show; listVisions via stacks.readBook for the where-clause expressiveness.

decisions.rationale: clerk.list({ type: 'vision' }) already does the right thing for typed listing (mandate-implicit-filter only fires when phase is supplied without type — see clerk/types.ts:223-251 docstring). For stage filters, the where-clause filtering against ext.cartograph.stage isn't directly expressible through clerk.list (it accepts WritFilters, not WhereClause), so listX would post-filter in memory after clerk.list. This matches reckoner's post-filter pattern. clerk.show is the natural single-row primitive; cartograph already uses it in tools/render.ts. Default to extending the existing typed surface.

decisions.question: How does listX implement the stage filter — query Stacks with a where-clause on ext.cartograph.stage, or post-filter in memory after clerk.list({ type })?
decisions.context: Stacks supports dot-notation where clauses (tier4-edge-cases.ts:234-247) including against unindexed fields (json_extract scan in SQLite, getNestedField in memory). No index exists on ext.cartograph.stage in clerk's writs-book schema. clerk.list returns WritDoc[]; cartograph would still need to project to {id, stage, codex, createdAt, updatedAt}.

options.postFilterInMemory: Call clerk.list({ type: 'vision' }), then filter the returned array by entry.ext?.cartograph?.stage === stage in memory. Mirrors reckoner's pattern.
options.stacksReadBookWithWhere: Bypass clerk.list. Call stacks.readBook<WritDoc>('clerk','writs').find({ where: [['type','=','vision'], ['ext.cartograph.stage','=',stage]], orderBy: ['createdAt','desc'], limit, offset }). One query, declarative.

decisions.rationale: Reckoner precedent (tick.ts:266-272) — query by indexed field (type), post-filter on ext.<plugin>.* in memory. Keeps the typed surface routing through clerk.list (matches D3). Stage filtering at the Stacks layer is technically possible but would defeat the point of D3 (route through clerk's typed surface). Filter selectivity is high (cartograph carves writs by type first; ~6 stage values per type), so the in-memory filter is small. If perf becomes a concern, observation O1 captures the index/post-filter design question for the broader ext-slot pattern.

decisions.question: Where does codex live as the source of truth — only in writ.codex (clerk's native field), only in ext.cartograph.codex (per brief §1), or in both kept in sync?
decisions.context: Brief §1 explicitly defines `interface CartographExt { stage; codex? }` and brief §4 says the projection reads `writ.ext['cartograph'].codex`. But clerk.post natively accepts a codex field that lands at writ.codex (visible to writ-list / writ-show / clerk.edit). If cartograph only writes ext.cartograph.codex and skips writ.codex, the cartograph view diverges from the writ view; if cartograph writes both, the values must be kept in sync. clerk.edit allows codex changes only when the writ is in 'new' state — so post-creation codex updates via patchVision({codex}) would have to bypass clerk.edit (e.g. via direct stacks.patch or via clerk's edit API which would reject).

options.extOnly: ext.cartograph.codex is authoritative; writ.codex is set on creation but never re-synced post-creation. Projection reads from ext.
options.writOnly: Drop codex from CartographExt entirely. Projection reads writ.codex. patchVision({codex}) would need to bypass clerk.edit's 'new'-state restriction (write directly via stacks).
options.bothSyncedAtomically: On createX and patchX({codex}), write both writ.codex (via clerk.post for create or direct stacks.patch for post-create updates) and ext.cartograph.codex (via setWritExt). Inside one stacks.transaction so they never diverge. Projection reads from ext per brief.

decisions.rationale: Brief §1 is explicit: `{ stage, codex }`, so codex stays in CartographExt — option B is ruled out. Between A and C: option A allows the cartograph view and the writ view to drift on post-create patchVision({codex}) calls (a real path used in vision-apply.ts:390-394 today). Option C costs one extra direct stacks.patch but keeps writ-list / writ-show / cartograph-show consistent. Three Defaults: prefer fail-loud over silent fallback — silent codex divergence is a fallback we'd rather not allow. The dual-write is small and lives inside the cartograph's transaction boundary.

decisions.question: Does patchX({stage}) — direct stage edits without phase change — remain supported as a typed-API path?
decisions.context: Today, patchVision({stage: 'X'}) writes the doc's stage field directly without touching writ.phase. This is the rare path vision-apply.ts:408-411 uses when phase didn't change but stage drifted (cartograph.ts:353-358 implements patchVision via this.visionsBook.patch). The brief's commitment is the typed-API stays stable — argument shapes preserved. The semantics of patchX({stage}) are debatable: it intentionally bypasses transition's coupling.

options.preserveSemantics: patchX({stage: 'X'}) updates ext.cartograph.stage via setWritExt without touching writ.phase. Mirrors today's behavior; vision-apply.ts call site keeps working unchanged.
options.removeStagePath: Reject {stage} in patchX. All stage transitions must go through transitionX. Update vision-apply.ts:408-411 to use transitionX with phase=writ.phase (which transitionX would need to relax to allow no-phase-change transitions) OR use a new direct-stage-write helper.

decisions.rationale: Brief commits 'argument shapes... stay'. patchX accepts Partial<Omit<TDoc, 'id'>>, which includes stage. Removing stage from the patch surface is a behavior change beyond the brief's scope. The known divergence path (vision-apply's stage-drifted-without-phase-drifted case) is rare but real, and the implementation is one setWritExt call away. Three Defaults: prefer the simpler answer; preserving semantics is the lower-risk move.

decisions.question: Should the cartograph apparatus continue to declare books in supportKit, or remove the books contribution entirely?
decisions.context: Current cartograph.ts:681-686 declares three books in supportKit.books with stage/codex/createdAt indexes. Post-cleanup the books are not used at all — projection reads from writ.ext. The supportKit.books contribution is the kit-level interface that tells the framework to ensure these books exist at startup.

options.removeAllThree: Remove the entire supportKit.books property. The cartograph contributes only writ types (via clerk.registerWritType) and tools.
options.keepEmptyBooks: Keep supportKit.books as an empty object. Defensive against future re-introduction.
options.keepBooksAsDeprecated: Keep declaring the books but mark them deprecated.

decisions.rationale: Three Defaults: prefer removal to deprecation. The brief explicitly says 'cartograph/visions, cartograph/charges, and cartograph/pieces Stacks books are removed entirely.' No external consumer reads the books. Keeping them empty would just confuse a reader.

decisions.question: Should the new CartographExt sub-slot type be exported as a public type from the package's public surface, or kept module-internal?
decisions.context: Brief §1 defines the slot's payload shape but doesn't say whether to export it. Reckoner exports its ReckonerExt publicly (packages/plugins/reckoner/src/types.ts:117). Cartograph's slot is simpler ({ stage, codex }) and arguably internal — no other plugin should be writing to ext['cartograph'].

options.exportPublic: Export CartographExt from index.ts so consumers (e.g. future surveyor) can read the slot in a typed way.
options.moduleInternal: Keep CartographExt as a private type in cartograph.ts. Consumers project from writ.ext['cartograph'] using a structural cast.

decisions.rationale: Reckoner precedent — public ext types make slot reads typed for downstream consumers. Commission C (the surveyor substrate, c-moji0ggh) will need to read the cartograph node's stage to drive its CDC observer; an exported type makes that clean. The type is small and documents the slot contract.

decisions.question: Should the constant string 'cartograph' (the plugin id used as the ext sub-slot key) be hoisted into a named constant CARTOGRAPH_PLUGIN_ID, or repeated as a string literal at each call site?
decisions.context: Reckoner uses a module-local const RECKONER_PLUGIN_ID = 'reckoner' (reckoner.ts:132 and tick.ts:99-100). Clerk exports its own CLERK_PLUGIN_ID publicly (clerk/types.ts:82).

options.namedConstant: Define const CARTOGRAPH_PLUGIN_ID = 'cartograph' in cartograph.ts (or the new types.ts public surface). Use everywhere the slot key is referenced.
options.stringLiterals: Repeat 'cartograph' as a literal at each call site. Three or four sites total.

decisions.rationale: Reckoner precedent. A typo on a literal-string slot key fails silently (the read returns undefined). A named constant fails at compile time. Cheap consistency.

decisions.question: Should the [key: string]: unknown index signature on VisionDoc / ChargeDoc / PieceDoc projection types be retained or removed once they no longer need to satisfy BookEntry?
decisions.context: Today, the index signature is required so the doc types can be Stacks BookEntry-compatible (cartograph.test.ts and the apparatus pass these to stacks.book<VisionDoc>). Post-cleanup, the docs are projection shapes only — never stored in a Stacks book. Brief §6: 'The existing VisionDoc / ChargeDoc / PieceDoc exported types stay as projection shapes (their definitions can be retained verbatim — the only difference is where the data comes from).'

options.retainVerbatim: Keep the index signature for forward-compat (callers can still extend the projection). Update the docstring to reflect 'projection extension' rather than BookEntry.
options.dropIndexSignature: Remove the index signature. The projection types become closed shapes.

decisions.rationale: Brief explicitly says 'definitions can be retained verbatim'. Concurrent doc update on the docstring (which says 'required to satisfy BookEntry') is implied. Forward-compat for projection extension is a cheap win.

decisions.question: Should the cartograph's writs book gain an index on ext.cartograph.stage to make stage-filtered listX queries efficient, or accept full-table json_extract scan?
decisions.context: Adding the index is a cross-package change — the writs book is owned and registered by the clerk apparatus (clerk.ts:1241-1243). Cartograph cannot extend clerk's supportKit. Stacks supports indexed queries on dot-notation fields (tier4-edge-cases.ts:234-247). With ~tens-to-hundreds of vision/charge/piece writs early on, full scan is fine; at scale, an index helps but the scaling concern is broader (multiple plugins use ext sub-slots).

options.noIndex: Accept full-table json_extract scan. Cartograph and other ext-slot consumers (reckoner, future surveyor) all scan today.
options.addClerkIndex: Modify clerk's supportKit.books.writs.indexes to add ['type','ext.cartograph.stage']. Cross-package change.
options.deferToObservation: No index added now; lift the cross-cutting question 'how should plugins index their ext sub-slots' as an observation.

decisions.rationale: At expected v0 scale (early cartograph adoption), filter cardinality is low and total row count is small. No index is the simplest answer. Cross-cutting design — adding one cartograph-specific index to clerk's writs book sets a bad precedent (every plugin would do the same, polluting clerk's schema). Treat as part of D4's post-filter approach. Lift the broader 'how to index ext sub-slots cleanly' question as observation O1.

decisions.question: When a CDC test asserts CDC event counts, should the watcher migrate to watching the writs book filtered by entry.type === 'vision', or should the assertion be dropped entirely?
decisions.context: Three test sites count CDC events on cartograph/visions today (cartograph.test.ts:342-364, vision-apply.test.ts:159-166 + many assertions, vision-apply.test.ts:521-538). The asserted property — 'one CDC event per logical change' — is structurally the same post-cleanup, just observed on the writs book filtered by writ type. The brief mentions 'one CDC-significant phase transition per apply' as the substrate's invariant.

options.migrateWatcher: Replace stacks.watch<VisionDoc>('cartograph','visions',...) with stacks.watch<WritDoc>('clerk','writs', evt => { if (evt.entry?.type === 'vision') ... }). Translate counts.
options.dropAssertions: Remove the CDC-counting assertions; rely on the writs-book CDC pipeline being correct end-to-end.

decisions.rationale: The invariant is real — single-event-per-apply protects the substrate from doubled survey writs (per surveying-cascade.md §3.6 update). Dropping the assertions loses regression coverage at the exact place it matters most. Migration is mechanical: change the book + filter. Three Defaults: don't drop test coverage silently.

decisions.question: Vision-apply currently writes ext['surveyor'] separately after cartograph.createVision returns (vision-apply.ts:347, 420). Should vision-apply additionally wrap cartograph.createVision + clerk.setWritExt(SURVEYOR_PLUGIN_ID, ...) in a single outer stacks.transaction so the entire apply produces one CDC event on the writs book (post-coalescing)?
decisions.context: Today: cartograph.createVision opens its own tx (1 cartograph-visions create + 1 writs-book create), then setWritExt(surveyor) runs separately (1 writs-book update). The cartograph-visions test counts visions-book events; the writs-book gets two events but tests don't assert on that. Post-cleanup: the cartograph-visions book is gone. cartograph.createVision produces 1 writs-book create (coalesced), and the separate surveyor stamp produces 1 writs-book update — total 2 writs-book events per first-apply. Wrapping both in one outer stacks.transaction would coalesce them to 1 writs-book create event. The substrate's single-event-per-apply guarantee (per surveying-cascade.md §3.6) is at stake.

options.wrapInVisionApply: vision-apply opens an outer stacks.transaction(...) covering both cartograph.createVision and the surveyor setWritExt. Both join via flattening; coalescing yields one writs-book create event per first-apply.
options.dontWrap: Leave vision-apply's two writes as separate operations. The writs book sees create+update per first-apply. Substrate observers (when built) deal with the dedup via writ id within a short window — surveying-cascade.md §3.6 already specifies a defensive dedup.

decisions.rationale: The brief's update to surveying-cascade.md §3.6 says 'the cartograph's createX/transitionX primitives are already transactional' with the same outcome (one CDC-significant phase transition per apply). Vision-apply is the call site that bundles both writes for a single logical patron operation; the substrate's single-event guarantee depends on the call-site producing one event. The defensive dedup in surveying-cascade.md §3.6 is a backstop, not a primary guarantee. Wrapping is one stacks.transaction wrapper around a small block in vision-apply.ts. Three Defaults: prefer fail-loud / explicit atomicity over relying on a downstream dedup.

decisions.question: Should patchVision's redundant updatedAt: new Date().toISOString() patch payload (vision-patch.ts:37) be removed, since setWritExt now bumps updatedAt itself?
decisions.context: Today's vision-patch.ts handler passes { codex, updatedAt: new Date().toISOString() } to cartograph.patchVision. Post-cleanup, the patch routes through clerk.setWritExt which bumps the writ's updatedAt automatically. The manual updatedAt becomes dead code.

options.remove: Drop the manual updatedAt argument. setWritExt sets it.

decisions.rationale: Three Defaults: prefer removal. The manual updatedAt was needed when the doc was a separate row (the doc's updatedAt and writ's updatedAt were different fields). Post-cleanup they're the same field, set by setWritExt. Dead code removal is a small concurrent cleanup.

decisions.question: How thorough should the surveying-cascade arch doc rewrites be — exactly the three named sections (§3.4, §3.6, §3.7), or a broader sweep including incidental references in §2 (mermaid diagram), §3.1 (vision-apply pipeline), and Phase 2 of the worked example (§4.2)?
decisions.context: Brief §6 names §3.4, §3.6, §3.7 explicitly and adds 'minor incidental references elsewhere in the doc may need to be touched up for consistency.' Incidental references that mention the companion-book CDC: line 66 (mermaid 'emits Stacks CDC: book.cartograph.visions.{created,updated}'), line 466 (worked example 'CDC fires once per the single-event-per-apply guarantee'), and the SurveyDoc bullet at line 44 of the vocabulary table.

options.namedSectionsOnly: Update only §3.4, §3.6, §3.7 verbatim per the brief's prescription. Leave incidental references for a follow-up pass.
options.thoroughSweep: Update the three named sections plus all incidental references — line 66 mermaid arrow, line 44 vocab table SurveyDoc entry, line 466 worked-example CDC reference. Single coherent doc when the commission lands.

decisions.rationale: Brief explicitly permits 'minor incidental references elsewhere... touched up for consistency.' Doc drift on the same touched file is concurrent doc updates needed (per the inventory). Patron's stated motivation (Commission C briefed against post-cleanup shape) is undermined by leaving the mermaid diagram still saying 'book.cartograph.visions.{created,updated}'. Three Defaults: prefer the complete answer.

observations.title: Standardize ext sub-slot indexing strategy across plugins
observations.body:
Multiple apparatuses now own `writ.ext['<plugin>']` sub-slots (reckoner, surveyor-future, cartograph after this commission). All of them filter writs by sub-slot fields (e.g. reckoner reads `ext.reckoner.source`, cartograph reads `ext.cartograph.stage`). The current pattern is to query by an indexed top-level field (`type` or `phase`) and post-filter in memory.

At scale this is a real perf concern, but the cross-cutting design question is also real: clerk's `writs` book schema lives in `packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.ts:1241-1247`, which means every plugin that wants an indexed query against its ext sub-slot needs clerk to know about it. Plugin-specific indexes in clerk's schema are an ownership inversion. Reasonable resolutions:

- Accept the post-filter pattern as the canonical one (current state) and document it.
- Extend `ClerkApi` (or a kit-contribution surface) so plugins can declaratively contribute indexes against `ext.<plugin>.<field>` for the writs book without clerk needing per-plugin awareness.
- Provide a `ClerkApi.findByExt(pluginId, field, value)` primitive that abstracts the index/scan choice.

This is not blocking the cartograph cleanup (D11 selects no-index post-filter for now), but as ext-slot adoption grows it will need a substrate-level answer. Surface as a design click for the broader plugin-ecosystem track.

Files to consider: `packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.ts:1241-1247` (writs book schema), `packages/plugins/clerk/src/types.ts:559+` (ClerkApi surface), `packages/plugins/reckoner/src/tick.ts:247-272` (current post-filter pattern), `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/cartograph.ts` (post-cleanup, will be a second precedent).

observations.title: Document and codify the nested-stacks-transaction-flattens contract
observations.body:
`packages/plugins/stacks/src/stacks-core.ts:191-196` implements transaction flattening (nested `stacks.transaction(...)` calls reuse the outer transaction). This behavior is critical to the cartograph cleanup (and to clerk.setWritExt's atomicity-with-arbitrary-callers): without it, every external `clerk.setWritExt` or `clerk.post` call would fail to atomically join an outer transaction, and we'd need to extend ClerkApi with `tx`-context-accepting variants of every primitive.

The flattening behavior is documented only by a one-line code comment (`// If already in a transaction, just run (no nesting — flattened)`). It is not surfaced in the public StacksApi documentation, the README, or `docs/architecture/`. Anyone reasoning about cartograph's post-cleanup atomicity (or surveyor's, when it lands; or any future plugin combining clerk + setWritExt + transition under one boundary) needs to know this is the contract.

Lift as a design clarification commission: document the flattening contract in `packages/plugins/stacks/README.md` and/or in the StacksApi docstring on `transaction()` (`packages/plugins/stacks/src/types.ts:175`). Optionally surface in the cross-package-coupling architecture doc.

The substrate behavior is correct and load-bearing; the gap is documentation, not implementation. This is real because every plugin that combines clerk's transactional operations is implicitly betting on flattening — without docs, this is a hidden coupling waiting to surprise someone.

content.text:
All four artifacts written. Here's a summary of what I produced for the cartograph `ext['cartograph']` collapse plan:

**Inventory** — Full map of the 17 cartograph source files, the 3 test files, the surveying-cascade.md sections to update, and the load-bearing primitives the new code consumes (clerk.setWritExt, stacks.transaction nesting flattening, dot-notation where queries, clerk.transition's silent ext-strip). External consumer count: **zero** — blast radius is genuinely contained to the cartograph package + the one architecture doc.

**Scope** — Two items, both included:
- **S1**: The storage migration itself (book → ext sub-slot), including all consequent test/README updates.
- **S2**: The surveying-cascade arch doc rewrites (§3.4, §3.6, §3.7 + incidental refs).

**Decisions** — 15 decisions, all pre-filled with `selected`:
- Brief-pre-empted: D1 (atomicity wrapper), D7 (remove books), D10 (retain index sig).
- Implementation-shape: D2 (delegate createX to clerk.post via tx flattening), D3 (project via clerk.show/list), D4 (post-filter stage in memory), D11 (no new index).
- Edge cases: D5 (sync writ.codex + ext.cartograph.codex), D6 (preserve patchX({stage}) divergence path), D12 (migrate CDC watcher, don't drop), D13 (vision-apply wraps cartograph create + surveyor stamp in one tx for single-event guarantee), D14 (drop redundant updatedAt).
- Public-surface: D8 (export CartographExt), D9 (named CARTOGRAPH_PLUGIN_ID constant).
- Doc thoroughness: D15 (sweep incidental references, not just the three named sections).

**Observations** — 2 records:
- **obs-1**: Standardize ext sub-slot indexing strategy across plugins (cross-cutting design Q as ext adoption grows).
- **obs-2**: Document the nested-stacks-transaction-flattens contract (load-bearing primitive with only a one-line code comment as documentation).

[
  {
    "type": "text",
    "text": "{
  "id": "w-mop8jfg4-e4666f5317bb",
  "codex": "x018-with-surface-map-tight-cartograph-b8b8b71c",
